attributes.stem: A 13-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with his mother. She found him in his room surrounded by several empty packets of immediate release paracetamol. It is unclear how many tablets he took, but the patient states that he ingested them five hours ago. He has a history of depression but takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. He is alert and oriented.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Referral to the liver transplant team
attributes.explanation:
In this case of suspected paracetamol overdose, the most appropriate next step is to obtain a serum paracetamol level. The crucial aspect of this scenario is the timeframe of ingestion. For immediate release paracetamol, even with an unknown dose, it is the serum paracetamol concentration between 4 and 8 hours post-ingestion plotted on a nomogram that determines the indication for N-acetylcysteine (NAC) treatment.

If the patient presents less than 4 hours post-ingestion, the level is not done until the 4-hour mark. While NAC treatment within 8 hours of ingestion is crucial in preventing fulminant hepatic failure, it is safe to wait for a paracetamol level as long as the result is expected to be available by the 8-hour mark. In this case, at 5 hours post-ingestion, there is still time to obtain and act on the serum level.

Activated charcoal may be considered in presentations within two to four hours of ingestion but is of little benefit beyond this time. At 5 hours post-ingestion, it would not be effective in this case.

Referral to the liver transplant team may be warranted in severely unwell patients, but not in this patient who appears well. Indications for liver transplant referral include persistent acidosis, encephalopathy, or elevated INR.

N-acetylcysteine infusion is the definitive treatment in paracetamol overdose. However, it is only indicated when the serum paracetamol concentration between 4-8 hours post-ingestion is above the nomogram line. Without a serum level, initiating NAC would be premature at this stage.

Gastric lavage is not indicated in paracetamol overdose, especially not at 5 hours post-ingestion. It is generally only considered within 1 hour of ingestion of a potentially life-threatening amount of a toxin.

It's important to note that management of sustained-release paracetamol overdose is different and requires consideration of total dose and two serum paracetamol concentrations taken 4 hours apart. However, this case specifically mentions immediate release paracetamol.

After obtaining the serum paracetamol level, the next steps would depend on the result. If the level is above the treatment line on the nomogram, NAC infusion would be initiated. Regardless of the paracetamol level, this patient will require a mental health assessment given his history of depression and the intentional nature of the overdose.
learningPoints: It is safe to delay N-acetylcysteine administration up to 8 hours post-ingestion of immediate release paracetamol if it is possible to obtain a serum paracetamol level between 4-8 hours post-ingestion.
Serum paracetamol concentration between 4 and 8 hours post-ingestion plotted on a nomogram determines the indication for N-acetylcysteine treatment in immediate release paracetamol overdose.
references: Emergency Care Institute, New South Wales, 2020. Paracetamol overdose. https://aci.health.nsw.gov.au/networks/eci/clinical/clinical-tools/toxicology/paracetamol
The Royal Children’s Hospital, Melbourne, 2018. Clinical Practice Guidelines: paracetamol poisoning.
South Australian Paediatric Clinical Practice Guidelines, 2018, Paracetamol Poisoning
